topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rejection email sat glowing on the screen, a stark white rectangle against the muted backdrop of my dorm room. I stared at the words—'regret to inform you'—as if they were a physical assault. My hands, which had been fidgeting with a worn textbook just moments before, now felt heavy and unresponsive. A slow, dragging sensation started in my chest, a dull ache that spread outwards. I traced the edges of the screen with a fingertip, the plastic cool beneath my skin. I didn't move to close the laptop. Instead, I pushed my chair back with a scraping sound that seemed deafening in the quiet room. The air felt thick, suffocating. I stood up, walked to the window, and watched the bustling campus life below. Every laugh, every hurried stride, seemed impossibly distant, a world operating on a frequency I was no longer tuned into.

[story 2]

I reread the rejection letter for the tenth time, the ink blurring slightly under my anxious gaze. My jaw tightened until a dull throb radiated across my cheeks. A bitter taste coated the back of my throat. I picked up my pen, intending to scribble some angry, nonsensical words on a scrap of paper, but my hand shook too violently. It dropped onto the desk with a soft thud. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning in protest, and closed my eyes. I could feel the tension coiling tight in my shoulders, a knot refusing to loosen. For a long time, there was only the sound of my own shallow breathing, each inhale feeling like a conscious effort, each exhale a surrender to the weight settling in my stomach.

[story 3]

The phone call had ended ten minutes ago, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I was sitting on the edge of my bed, staring blankly at the wall where a poster of the university crest hung. My energy felt utterly depleted, as if someone had drained the very substance from my bones. I kept rubbing my temples, a futile attempt to soothe the throbbing behind my eyes. Every small noise—the distant hum of the ventilation, the rustle of a passing student—felt amplified, grating against my nerves. I shifted my weight repeatedly, finding no comfortable position, only a restless, vibrating energy that demanded movement but refused to translate into action. I just sat there, suspended in a state of profound stillness, waiting for something to break.
